text.title: django-project-structure Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for django-project-structure. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Organize code into domain apps under apps/. Each app represents one business domain. Each app MUST contain: models.py, services.py, selectors.py, apis.py, serializers.py, urls.py, constants.py, admin.py, and a tests/ subdirectory with test_models.py, test_services.py, test_selectors.py, and test_apis.py.
rule.rationale: Domain-driven structure makes it possible to find all code for a concept in one folder. When an agent or developer needs to change how orders work, they look in apps/orders/ — not scattered across global models/, views/, and serializers/ directories.

rule.statement: Put shared infrastructure (BaseModel, custom exceptions, pagination, permissions) in core/. Put Django configuration (settings split by environment, root urls.py, celery.py, wsgi.py, asgi.py) in config/. Put test factories in tests/factories/ at the project root.
rule.rationale: core/ holds code that all apps import; it must not import from apps/ to avoid circular dependencies. config/ separates runtime concerns from domain logic. Centralized factories allow all test modules to reference them without duplication.

rule.statement: App directories use snake_case plural nouns (users, orders, user_profiles). Model classes use PascalCase singular (User, Order, UserProfile). Service functions use entity_action pattern (user_create, order_process). Selector functions use entity_query pattern (user_list, order_get_by_id). Constants use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE. TextChoices classes use PascalCase (OrderStatus).
rule.rationale: Consistent naming makes code searchable. A grep for user_create finds all create operations for users. Naming patterns signal intent: user_list is a read operation (selector), order_process is a write operation (service).
